examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize in_degree = [0,1,1,2], graph = {0:[1,2], 1:[3], 2:[3], 3:[]}, ordering = []. Step 2: Zero courses = [0] (in_degree[0]=0). Choose 0: ordering=[0], decrement in_degree of 1 and 2: in_degree=[0,0,0,2]. Step 3: Recursive call: zero courses = [1,2]. Choose 1: ordering=[0,1], decrement in_degree of 3: in_degree=[0,0,0,1]. Step 4: Recursive call: zero courses = [2]. Choose 2: ordering=[0,1,2], decrement in_degree of 3: in_degree=[0,0,0,0]. Step 5: Recursive call: zero courses = [3]. Choose 3: ordering=[0,1,2,3], length=4, return ordering. Backtracking not needed. Valid order: [0,1,2,3].

Step 1: in_degree = [1,1,1], graph = {0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[0]}, ordering = []. Step 2: Zero courses = [] (all in_degree >0). No course to choose, backtrack immediately. Since no valid ordering found, return [] indicating cycle.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS Backtracking
approaches.summary: This approach uses depth-first search to explore all possible orderings by recursively selecting courses with no prerequisites and backtracking if a cycle is detected. It is straightforward but inefficient for large graphs due to exponential time complexity.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n! * n) where n is the number of courses, due to generating all permutations and checking prerequisites for each.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for recursion stack and storing the current ordering.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of topological ordering using DFS backtracking to explore all permutations.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the course schedule ordering problem by treating it as a search through all possible permutations of courses, validating each against prerequisite constraints. The intuition is that we can recursively build orderings by selecting courses that have no remaining prerequisites at each step, backtracking when we encounter a cycle or dead end. We maintain a graph representation (adjacency list) of prerequisites, a visited set to detect cycles, and a current ordering list. At each recursive step, we identify courses with zero in-degree (no prerequisites left to take), add one to the ordering, decrement in-degrees of its dependents, and recurse. If we reach a state where no courses have zero in-degree but not all courses are placed, we've hit a cycle and backtrack. The base case is when all courses are placed, yielding a valid topological order. Time complexity is O(n! * n) because we generate all n! permutations and for each, check up to n prerequisites. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and auxiliary structures. This connects to L1 by implementing the full recursive backtracking framework that uses the cycle detection subroutine. Common mistakes include not properly restoring state during backtracking (leading to incorrect in-degree counts) and missing cycle detection in graphs with self-loops. Real code pattern: def dfs_backtrack(ordering, in_degree, graph): if len(ordering) == n: return ordering; zero_courses = [c for c in range(n) if in_degree[c] == 0 and c not in ordering]; for course in zero_courses: ordering.append(course); for neighbor in graph[course]: in_degree[neighbor] -= 1; result = dfs_backtrack(ordering, in_degree, graph); if result: return result; for neighbor in graph[course]: in_degree[neighbor] += 1; ordering.pop(); return None.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize in_degree = [0,1,1,2], graph = {0:[1,2], 1:[3], 2:[3], 3:[]}, ordering = []. Step 2: Zero courses = [0] (in_degree[0]=0). Choose 0: ordering=[0], decrement in_degree of 1 and 2: in_degree=[0,0,0,2]. Step 3: Recursive call: zero courses = [1,2]. Choose 1: ordering=[0,1], decrement in_degree of 3: in_degree=[0,0,0,1]. Step 4: Recursive call: zero courses = [2]. Choose 2: ordering=[0,1,2], decrement in_degree of 3: in_degree=[0,0,0,0]. Step 5: Recursive call: zero courses = [3]. Choose 3: ordering=[0,1,2,3], length=4, return ord
... (simplified)

Step 1: in_degree = [1,1,1], graph = {0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[0]}, ordering = []. Step 2: Zero courses = [] (all in_degree >0). No course to choose, backtrack immediately. Since no valid ordering found, return [] indicating cycle.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Cycle Detection / Zero In-degree Processing
ladders.desc: DFS traversal that detects cycles by tracking visited states (unvisited, visiting, visited).
ladders.explanation: This subroutine detects cycles in a directed graph, which is crucial for the brute force approach to backtrack when a dead end (cycle) is encountered. The intuition is that during DFS, if we revisit a node that is currently in the 'visiting' state (i.e., part of the current recursion path), we've found a cycle. We use three states: 0=unvisited, 1=visiting, 2=visited. Starting from each unvisited node, we mark it as visiting, recursively visit its neighbors, and if we encounter a neighbor in visiting state, return true for cycle detection. After processing all neighbors, mark as visited. Time complexity is O(V+E) for traversing all vertices and edges once. Space complexity is O(V) for state array and recursion stack. This feeds into L0 by providing the mechanism to abort invalid orderings early. Common mistakes include not resetting state properly or missing cycles in disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: def has_cycle(node, graph, state): if state[node] == 1: return True; if state[node] == 2: return False; state[node] = 1; for neighbor in graph[node]: if has_cycle(neighbor, graph, state): return True; state[node] = 2; return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: state = [0,0,0]. Call has_cycle(0): state[0]=1. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1: call has_cycle(1): state[1]=1. Step 3: Visit neighbor 2: call has_cycle(2): state[2]=1. Step 4: Visit neighbor 0: state[0]=1 (visiting), return true. Bubble up true, cycle detected.

Step 1: state = [0,0,0]. Call has_cycle(0): state[0]=1. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1: call has_cycle(1): state[1]=1. Step 3: Visit neighbor 2: call has_cycle(2): state[2]=1, no neighbors, state[2]=2, return false. Step 4: Back to node 1: state[1]=2, return false. Step 5: Back to node 0: state[0]=2, return false. No cycle.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dependency Resolution / Post-order Accumulation
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of courses while checking prerequisite constraints at each step.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves recursively building a permutation (ordering) of courses, but only extending it with courses that satisfy prerequisite constraints at that moment. The intuition is to treat the problem as a search tree where each node represents a partial ordering, and we branch on courses that have no prerequisites left (in-degree zero). At each recursive call, we identify available courses, choose one, add it to the ordering, update in-degrees of its dependents, and recurse. If recursion leads to a dead end (no available courses but ordering incomplete), we backtrack by restoring state. This ensures we explore all valid permutations. Time complexity is O(n! * n) in worst case as we may generate all n! orderings. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and current ordering. This builds on L3 by using in-degree calculation to enforce constraints. Common mistakes include not pruning branches early (leading to exponential blow-up) and incorrect state restoration. Real code pattern: def backtrack(ordering, in_degree, graph): if len(ordering) == n: yield ordering.copy(); available = [c for c in range(n) if in_degree[c] == 0 and c not in ordering]; for course in available: ordering.append(course); for neighbor in graph[course]: in_degree[neighbor] -= 1; backtrack(ordering, in_degree, graph); for neighbor in graph[course]: in_degree[neighbor] += 1; ordering.pop().

examples.trace: Step 1: available=[0]. Choose 0: ordering=[0], update in_degree to [0,0,0]. Step 2: Recursive call: available=[1,2]. Choose 1: ordering=[0,1], in_degree unchanged. Step 3: Recursive call: available=[2]. Choose 2: ordering=[0,1,2], length=3, yield [0,1,2]. Step 4: Backtrack: pop 2, restore in_degree. Step 5: Back to step 2: choose 2 instead: ordering=[0,2], in_degree unchanged. Step 6: Recursive call: available=[1]. Choose 1: ordering=[0,2,1], yield [0,2,1]. All permutations generated.

Step 1: available=[1] (in_degree[1]=0). Choose 1: ordering=[1], update in_degree of neighbor 0: in_degree=[0,0]. Step 2: Recursive call: available=[0]. Choose 0: ordering=[1,0], length=2, yield [1,0]. No other branches.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Construction
ladders.desc: Calculate the number of incoming edges (prerequisites) for each node in a graph.
ladders.explanation: This building block computes the in-degree for each vertex in a directed graph, which represents how many prerequisites a course has. The intuition is that in-degree zero nodes are candidates to start or continue a topological order. Given an adjacency list representation (graph), we initialize an in-degree array of size n with zeros. For each edge (u,v) in prerequisites, indicating v depends on u (u must come before v), we increment in_degree[v] by 1. This directly models prerequisite constraints. Time complexity is O(E) for iterating through all edges. Space complexity is O(V) for the in-degree array. This feeds into L2 by providing the constraint data needed for backtracking. Common mistakes include misinterpreting edge direction or not handling duplicate edges. Real code pattern: def compute_indegree(n, prerequisites): in_degree = [0]*n; for prereq in prerequisites: u, v = prereq; in_degree[v] += 1; return in_degree.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize in_degree = [0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Process [1,0]: increment in_degree[0]? No, edge is (1,0) meaning 0->1, so increment in_degree[1] by 1: in_degree=[0,1,0,0]. Step 3: Process [2,0]: increment in_degree[0]? No, increment in_degree[2] by 1: in_degree=[0,1,1,0]. Step 4: Process [3,1]: increment in_degree[1]? No, increment in_degree[3] by 1: in_degree=[0,1,1,1]. Step 5: Process [3,2]: increment in_degree[2]? No, increment in_degree[3] by 1: in_degree=[0,1,1,2]. Final: [0,1,1,2].

Step 1: in_degree = [0,0,0]. Step 2: Process [0,1]: increment in_degree[1] by 1: in_degree=[0,1,0]. Step 3: Process [1,2]: increment in_degree[2] by 1: in_degree=[0,1,1]. Wait, correction: edge (0,1) means 1 depends on 0, so in_degree[1]++. Edge (1,2) means 2 depends on 1, so in_degree[2]++. Yes, final: [0,1,1]. Actually, output should be [0,1,1]. Let's recalc: n=3, edges: 0->1, 1->2. in_degree[0]=0, in_degree[1]=1 (from edge 0->1), in_degree[2]=1 (from edge 1->2). So output [0,1,1]. My earlier trace had error, correct is [0,1,1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration / In-degree Updates
ladders.desc: Traverse a graph represented as an adjacency list to access neighbors of a node.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing and iterating over the neighbors of a node in a graph stored as an adjacency list. The intuition is that for many graph algorithms, we need to process each edge efficiently. An adjacency list is a dictionary or list of lists where index i contains a list of nodes that i points to (neighbors). To iterate, we simply loop through the list for a given node. For example, in Python-style: for neighbor in graph[node]: process(neighbor). This allows O(degree(node)) time per node. Space complexity is O(V+E) for storing the graph. This feeds into L3 by enabling in-degree computation and later DFS traversals. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds or misinterpreting directed vs undirected edges. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v). Then to iterate: for v in graph[u]: print(v).

examples.trace: Step 1: Access graph[0] = [1,2]. Step 2: Iterate: first neighbor 1, process it. Step 3: Next neighbor 2, process it. Step 4: End of list.
Step 1: Access graph[1] = [2]. Step 2: Iterate: neighbor 2, process it. Step 3: End of list.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs & Dependencies
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of directed graphs, dependencies, and topological ordering.
ladders.explanation: A directed graph models prerequisites where an edge u→v means course u must be taken before course v. Topological ordering arranges courses so all prerequisites come before dependent courses. If a cycle exists, no valid ordering is possible.

examples.trace: Step 1: Create list for 3 vertices: graph = [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge 0->1: graph[0].append(1) so graph[0]=[1]. Step 3: Add edge 0->2: graph[0].append(2) so graph[0]=[1,2]. Step 4: Vertices 1 and 2 have no outgoing edges, so lists remain empty. Final: graph = [[1,2],[],[]].
Step 1: graph = [[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge 0->1: graph[0].append(1) so graph[0]=[1]. Step 3: Add edge 1->0: graph[1].append(0) so graph[1]=[0]. Final: graph = [[1],[0]].

approaches.name: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS)
approaches.summary: This method uses Kahn's algorithm, which repeatedly removes nodes with zero in-degree (no prerequisites) using a queue, adding them to the ordering and updating in-degrees of neighbors. It efficiently detects cycles and builds a topological sort in linear time.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(V + E) where V is the number of courses and E is the number of prerequisites.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(V + E) for storing the graph adjacency list, in-degree array, and queue.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Kahn's Algorithm (BFS)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of topological sort using Kahn's algorithm with BFS to find valid course ordering.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding a valid topological ordering of courses given prerequisites, or detecting if no such ordering exists due to cycles. Kahn's algorithm provides an efficient O(V+E) solution by repeatedly removing nodes with zero in-degree. The intuition is that courses without prerequisites can be taken first, and as we take them, we 'free up' their dependent courses. We maintain an adjacency list to represent prerequisites, an in-degree array counting prerequisites for each course, and a queue to process zero in-degree nodes. At each step, we dequeue a node, add it to the result, and decrement in-degrees of its neighbors. If a neighbor's in-degree becomes zero, we enqueue it. If the result contains all nodes, we have a valid topological order; otherwise, there's a cycle. The algorithm works because topological sort requires linear ordering where all edges point forward, and zero in-degree nodes have no unresolved dependencies. Time complexity is O(V+E) for building the graph and processing each node/edge once. Space is O(V+E) for adjacency list, plus O(V) for in-degree array and queue. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of processing zero in-degree nodes into the complete solution. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize in-degree array properly, not handling empty input, or incorrect cycle detection by comparing result length. Real code pattern: Build adjacency list and in-degree array, initialize queue with zero in-degree nodes, process queue while updating in-degrees, return result if all nodes are included else empty list.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0: [1,2], 1: [3], 2: [3], 3: []}. In-degree: [0,1,1,2]. Queue: [0]. Result: [].
Step 2: Dequeue 0. Result: [0]. Update neighbors: in-degree[1]=0, in-degree[2]=0. Enqueue 1 and 2. Queue: [1,2].
Step 3: Dequeue 1. Result: [0,1]. Update neighbor 3: in-degree[3]=1. Queue: [2].
Step 4: Dequeue 2. Result: [0,1,2]. Update neighbor 3: in-degree[3]=0. Enqueue 3. Queue: [3].
Step 5: Dequeue 3. Result: [0,1,2,3]. Queue: [].
Step 6: Result length = 4 = numCourses, return [0,1,2,3].

Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0: [1], 1: [0]}. In-degree: [1,1]. Queue: [] (no zero in-degree nodes). Result: [].
Step 2: Queue empty initially, but we proceed to processing loop which won't execute.
Step 3: After loop, result length = 0 ≠ 2, return [] indicating cycle.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Cycle Detection / Zero In-degree Processing
ladders.desc: Implement the BFS queue processing that removes nodes with zero in-degree and updates their neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core engine of Kahn's algorithm, where we repeatedly process nodes that have no remaining prerequisites. The intuition is that nodes with zero in-degree are 'free' to be placed in the topological order, and removing them reduces in-degrees of dependent nodes, potentially making new nodes free. We maintain a queue of zero in-degree nodes. While the queue is not empty, we dequeue a node, add it to the result list, and for each neighbor in the adjacency list, decrement its in-degree. If a neighbor's in-degree becomes zero, we enqueue it. This ensures we process nodes in an order consistent with dependencies. The subroutine assumes we have precomputed adjacency list and in-degree array. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each node is enqueued/dequeued once and each edge is processed once. Space is O(V) for the queue. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called within the main algorithm after initialization. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of dependency resolution. Common mistakes include modifying the adjacency list while iterating (shouldn't), not checking for zero in-degree before enqueueing, or using a stack instead of queue (still works but changes order). Real code pattern: Initialize result list. While queue not empty: node = queue.pop(0); result.append(node); for neighbor in adj[node]: in_degree[neighbor] -= 1; if in_degree[neighbor] == 0: queue.append(neighbor).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Queue: [0]. Result: []. Dequeue 0, result=[0]. Neighbors: 1 and 2. in_degree[1]=0, enqueue 1. in_degree[2]=0, enqueue 2. Queue: [1,2].
Step 2: Dequeue 1, result=[0,1]. Neighbor 3: in_degree[3]=1. Queue: [2].
Step 3: Dequeue 2, result=[0,1,2]. Neighbor 3: in_degree[3]=0, enqueue 3. Queue: [3].
Step 4: Dequeue 3, result=[0,1,2,3]. Neighbors: none. Queue: []. Done.
Step 1: Queue is empty, so while loop doesn't execute. Result remains []. No changes to in_degree.

testCases.expected: result = [0,2,1,3], in_degree = [0,1,0,1] after step 1, then updates

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dependency Resolution / Post-order Accumulation
ladders.desc: Understand how decrementing in-degrees of neighbors models prerequisite completion.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the essence of topological sorting: when a node is processed (meaning we 'take' that course), all nodes that depend on it have one fewer prerequisite. We model this by decrementing the in-degree of each neighbor. The intuition is that in-degree represents the number of unresolved prerequisites; when it hits zero, the node becomes available for processing. This mechanism ensures we only process nodes when all their dependencies are satisfied, maintaining the topological order property. The logic is applied in the BFS loop: after adding a node to the result, for each neighbor, we reduce its in-degree by 1. If the new in-degree is zero, we add the neighbor to the queue. This step is where the algorithm resolves dependencies incrementally. Time complexity per operation is O(1) per edge, contributing to overall O(E). Space is O(1) extra per operation. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the neighbor-update loop within the queue processing. Connection to L3: It relies on the in-degree array built from the graph. Common mistakes include decrementing in-degree for nodes not in adjacency list, or not checking for zero after decrement. Real code pattern: for neighbor in adj[node]: in_degree[neighbor] -= 1; if in_degree[neighbor] == 0: queue.append(neighbor).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Process node 0. Neighbor 1: in_degree[1] was 1, decrement to 0. Since it's 0, enqueue 1.
Step 2: Neighbor 2: in_degree[2] was 1, decrement to 0. Enqueue 2.
Result: in_degree = [0,0,0], queue gains 1 and 2.

examples.output: in_degree becomes [0,0,1,1], node 3 not enqueued yet
examples.trace:
Step 1: Process node 1. Neighbor 3: in_degree[3] was 2, decrement to 1. Not zero, so no enqueue.
Result: in_degree = [0,0,1,1], queue unchanged for node 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Construction
ladders.desc: Create an array counting incoming edges for each node from the prerequisites list.
ladders.explanation: This building block computes the in-degree for each course, which is essential for Kahn's algorithm to identify nodes with zero prerequisites. The intuition is that in-degree represents how many courses must be taken before a given course. Given prerequisites as pairs [a,b] meaning course a requires course b (or equivalently, edge from b to a), we increment in-degree of a for each pair. We also build an adjacency list simultaneously for efficient neighbor access later. The process involves initializing an array of zeros with length numCourses, then iterating through prerequisites. For each pair [a,b], we add b to adj[a]'s list and increment in_degree[a] by 1. This step transforms the input into a graph representation. Time complexity is O(E) where E is number of prerequisites. Space is O(V+E) for adjacency list and O(V) for in-degree array. Connection to L2: The in-degree array is used in the dependency resolution logic. Connection to L4: It relies on iterating through a list of pairs. Common mistakes include confusing the direction of edges (should be from prerequisite to course), not initializing array size correctly, or missing courses with no prerequisites (they have in-degree 0). Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(numCourses)]; in_degree = [0]*numCourses; for a,b in prerequisites: adj[b].append(a); in_degree[a] += 1.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize adj = [[],[],[]], in_degree = [0,0,0].
Step 2: Process [1,0]: adj[0].append(1) → adj = [[1],[],[]]; in_degree[1] += 1 → [0,1,0].
Step 3: Process [2,0]: adj[0].append(2) → adj = [[1,2],[],[]]; in_degree[2] += 1 → [0,1,1].
Result: adj[0] = [1,2], others empty; in_degree shows courses 1 and 2 have one prerequisite each.

Step 1: Initialize adj = [[],[]], in_degree = [0,0].
Step 2: No prerequisites to process.
Result: All in-degrees zero, adjacency lists empty.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration / In-degree Updates
ladders.desc: Process a list of prerequisite pairs to extract course relationships.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves looping through the prerequisites array, where each element is a pair [a,b] indicating that course a depends on course b. The intuition is that we need to parse this input to understand dependencies between courses. In iteration, we access each pair and use its elements to build graph data structures. This is a fundamental step because the input format is given as a list of edges. We typically use a for-loop to traverse the list. For each pair, we can extract the dependent course and its prerequisite. This operation is simple but critical for transforming raw input into a usable graph representation. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of pairs. Space is O(1) extra per iteration if we process in place. Connection to L3: This iteration is used to construct the in-degree array and adjacency list. Connection to L5: It relies on understanding lists and loops. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors with course numbering (usually 0-indexed), misinterpreting pair order, or not handling empty list. Real code pattern: for prerequisite in prerequisites: a = prerequisite[0]; b = prerequisite[1]; # then use a and b.

examples.trace:
Step 1: First iteration: prerequisite = [1,0]. a = 1, b = 0.
Step 2: Second iteration: prerequisite = [2,0]. a = 2, b = 0.
Done.
The list is empty, so for-loop executes zero times.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs & Dependencies
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of directed graphs, dependencies, and topological ordering.
ladders.explanation: A directed graph models prerequisites where an edge u→v means course u must be taken before course v. Topological ordering arranges courses so all prerequisites come before dependent courses. If a cycle exists, no valid ordering is possible.

examples.trace: The pair [1,0] means the first number 1 is the dependent course, and the second number 0 is the prerequisite. So, to take course 1, you must complete course 0 first.

examples.output: Course 2 depends on course 1; course 3 depends on course 2
examples.trace:
First pair [2,1]: course 2 requires course 1.
Second pair [3,2]: course 3 requires course 2.
This implies a chain: take course 1, then 2, then 3.

testCases.expected: Course 1 depends on course 0; course 2 depends on course 0
Course 0 depends on course 1; course 1 depends on course 2
Course 2 depends on course 3; course 1 depends on course 2
Course 0 depends on itself; course 1 depends on itself

approaches.name: DFS Post-order Traversal
approaches.summary: This approach performs a depth-first search on the graph, marking nodes during traversal to detect cycles and recording nodes in post-order to reverse into a topological ordering. It leverages recursion and state tracking for a clean implementation.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(V + E) for traversing all nodes and edges in the graph.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(V + E) for adjacency list, recursion stack, and state arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — DFS Post-order Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find a valid topological ordering using DFS with post-order traversal and cycle detection.
ladders.explanation: This solution addresses the classic topological sorting problem where we need to find an ordering of courses such that all prerequisites are satisfied. The DFS with post-order traversal approach elegantly handles both cycle detection and ordering generation in a single pass. The intuition is that during DFS, we can mark nodes with three states: unvisited (0), visiting (1), and visited (2). When we encounter a node marked as 'visiting' during traversal, we've found a cycle. The post-order recording ensures that when we finish processing a node (after all its dependencies), we add it to our result list. Since DFS processes dependencies first, the final list is in reverse topological order, which we simply reverse at the end. The algorithm begins by building an adjacency list from the prerequisites. We then perform DFS on each unvisited node. During DFS, if we encounter a node marked as 'visiting', we've detected a cycle and return false. After processing all neighbors, we mark the node as 'visited' and add it to our result. The time complexity is O(V + E) where V is the number of courses and E is the number of prerequisites. Space complexity is O(V + E) for the adjacency list, recursion stack, and state array. This approach is particularly clean because it combines cycle detection and topological ordering in one algorithm, making it efficient and readable.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0: [], 1: [0], 2: [0], 3: [1,2]}. Step 2: Initialize state array: [0,0,0,0] and result list: []. Step 3: Start DFS from node 0. Node 0 has no neighbors, mark as visited (2), add to result: [0]. Step 4: DFS from node 1. Check neighbor 0 (visited), mark node 1 as visited, add to result: [0,1]. Step 5: DFS from node 2. Check neighbor 0 (visited), mark node 2 as visited, add to result: [0,1,2]. Step 6: DFS from node 3. Check neighbor 1 (visited), check neighbor 2 (visited), mark node 3 as visited, add to result: [0,1,
... (simplified)

Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0: [1], 1: [0]}. Step 2: Initialize state: [0,0], result: []. Step 3: Start DFS from node 0. Mark node 0 as visiting (1). Step 4: Process neighbor 1 of node 0. Node 1 is unvisited, start DFS on node 1. Mark node 1 as visiting. Step 5: Process neighbor 0 of node 1. Node 0 is marked as visiting (1) → cycle detected! Return false. Step 6: Backtrack, return false. No valid ordering exists, return empty list.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Cycle Detection / Zero In-degree Processing
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive DFS function that tracks node states to detect cycles and record post-order traversal.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the topological sorting algorithm. The DFS function takes a node as input and returns whether the subtree rooted at that node contains a cycle. It uses three states: 0 (unvisited), 1 (visiting), and 2 (visited). When we enter a node, we mark it as 'visiting' to indicate we're currently exploring its descendants. If we encounter a node already marked as 'visiting', we've found a back edge, indicating a cycle. After recursively processing all neighbors, we mark the node as 'visited' and record it in a result list. The intuition is that the 'visiting' state creates a temporary marker for nodes in the current recursion path, allowing us to detect cycles efficiently. The post-order recording (adding nodes after processing neighbors) naturally produces a reverse topological order because dependencies are added before the nodes that depend on them. Time complexity for this function is O(V + E) in total across all calls, as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the recursion stack and state array. This sub-routine connects to the full problem by being called for each unvisited node in the main function. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark nodes as 'visited' after processing (leading to infinite recursion) or incorrectly handling the cycle detection logic. The state tracking is crucial for both efficiency and correctness.

examples.trace: Step 1: Call dfs(0). Mark node 0 as visiting. Step 2: Process neighbor 1 of node 0. Call dfs(1). Mark node 1 as visiting. Step 3: Process neighbor 2 of node 1. Call dfs(2). Mark node 2 as visiting. Step 4: Node 2 has no neighbors, mark as visited, add to result: [2]. Step 5: Return to dfs(1). Mark node 1 as visited, add to result: [2,1]. Step 6: Return to dfs(0). Mark node 0 as visited, add to result: [2,1,0]. Step 7: Return true (no cycle).

Step 1: Call dfs(0). Mark node 0 as visiting. Step 2: Process neighbor 1 of node 0. Call dfs(1). Mark node 1 as visiting. Step 3: Process neighbor 0 of node 1. Node 0 is marked as visiting → cycle detected! Return false. Step 4: Backtrack, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dependency Resolution / Post-order Accumulation
ladders.desc: Understand how recording nodes after processing their dependencies produces a reverse topological order.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of this approach is that in a directed acyclic graph (DAG), if we record each node only after we've finished processing all its outgoing edges (dependencies), the resulting list will be in reverse topological order. This works because topological order requires that for every directed edge u→v, u comes before v in the ordering. During DFS, we explore v before finishing u (since u depends on v), so when we add u to the list after its DFS completes, all nodes reachable from u (including v) have already been added. Thus, u appears after v in our list, which is the reverse of the desired order. Reversing the list gives us the correct topological order. The intuition is similar to post-order traversal in trees, where we process children before parents. This logic naturally handles complex dependencies and ensures correctness as long as there are no cycles. The time complexity for building this list is O(V) since each node is added once. Space complexity is O(V) for the list. This feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the mechanism for accumulating nodes in the correct order. Common mistakes include adding nodes in pre-order (which doesn't guarantee topological order) or forgetting to reverse the final list. This technique is powerful because it decouples the traversal order from the final ordering requirement.

examples.trace: Step 1: DFS on node 0 (depends on 1 and 2). Step 2: Process neighbor 1 of node 0. DFS on node 1 (depends on 3). Step 3: Process neighbor 3 of node 1. DFS on node 3 (no dependencies). Add 3 to list: [3]. Step 4: Finish node 1, add to list: [3,1]. Step 5: Back to node 0, process neighbor 2. DFS on node 2 (depends on 3). Node 3 already visited, add node 2 to list: [3,1,2]. Step 6: Finish node 0, add to list: [3,1,2,0]. This is reverse topological order.

Step 1: DFS on node 0 (no dependencies). Add 0: [0]. Step 2: DFS on node 1 (depends on 0). Node 0 visited, add node 1: [0,1]. Step 3: DFS on node 2 (depends on 0). Node 0 visited, add node 2: [0,1,2]. Step 4: DFS on node 3 (depends on 1,2). Both visited, add node 3: [0,1,2,3]. Reverse order matches input dependencies.

testCases.expected: Reverse order: [3,1,0,2] or others depending on start node

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Construction
ladders.desc: Construct an adjacency list from course prerequisites for efficient graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: Before performing DFS, we need to represent the graph in a way that allows efficient neighbor access. An adjacency list is ideal for this problem because it provides O(1) access to a node's outgoing edges and uses O(V + E) space. Given numCourses (nodes) and prerequisites (edges), we build a list or dictionary where each node points to a list of nodes that depend on it (or vice versa, depending on direction). For topological sorting, we typically represent edges as u→v where u is a prerequisite for v, meaning v depends on u. In DFS, we traverse from v to u to process dependencies first. The adjacency list is built by iterating through prerequisites and adding each edge. The intuition is that this representation captures the dependency relationships in a compact form, enabling the DFS to efficiently explore the graph. Time complexity for building is O(E), and space is O(V + E). This feeds into the core logic by providing the graph structure needed for traversal. Common mistakes include incorrect edge direction (prerequisite vs dependent), not initializing entries for all nodes, or using inefficient representations like adjacency matrices. Proper graph representation is foundational for graph algorithms.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize list for 3 nodes: {0: [], 1: [], 2: []}. Step 2: Process [1,0]: course 1 depends on 0, so add 1 to adjacency list of 0 → {0: [1], 1: [], 2: []}. Step 3: Process [2,0]: course 2 depends on 0, so add 2 to adjacency list of 0 → {0: [1,2], 1: [], 2: []}. Step 4: All nodes accounted for.

Step 1: Initialize: {0: [], 1: [], 2: [], 3: []}. Step 2: Process [3,1]: 3 depends on 1, add 3 to list of 1 → {0: [], 1: [3], 2: [], 3: []}. Step 3: Process [3,2]: 3 depends on 2, add 3 to list of 2 → {0: [], 1: [3], 2: [3], 3: []}. Step 4: Process [1,0]: 1 depends on 0, add 1 to list of 0 → {0: [1], 1: [3], 2: [3], 3: []}. Step 5: Process [2,0]: 2 depends on 0, add 2 to list of 0 → {0: [1,2], 1: [3], 2: [3], 3: []}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration / In-degree Updates
ladders.desc: Perform depth-first search recursively to visit all nodes in a graph.
ladders.explanation: Depth-first search (DFS) is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. In the recursive version, we start at a node, mark it as visited, and then recursively call DFS on each of its unvisited neighbors. The intuition is to mimic exploring a maze by always taking the first available path until reaching a dead end, then backtracking. For topological sorting, DFS helps us explore dependencies deeply before processing the current node. The algorithm uses a visited set or array to avoid reprocessing nodes. Time complexity is O(V + E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the recursion stack and visited storage. This operation feeds into the building block by providing the traversal mechanism needed to process the adjacency list. Common mistakes include infinite recursion due to cycles (handled in higher levels), not marking nodes as visited before recursion, or stack overflow for large graphs. Recursive DFS is elegant but requires careful state management.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited. Step 2: Process neighbor 1 of node 0. Recurse to node 1, mark visited. Step 3: Process neighbor 3 of node 1. Recurse to node 3, mark visited. Step 4: Node 3 has no unvisited neighbors, backtrack to node 1. Step 5: Node 1 has no more unvisited neighbors, backtrack to node 0. Step 6: Process neighbor 2 of node 0. Recurse to node 2, mark visited. Step 7: Node 2 has neighbor 3 already visited, backtrack. Step 8: Backtrack to node 0, done.

Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited. Step 2: Process neighbor 1 of node 0. Recurse to node 1, mark visited. Step 3: Process neighbor 2 of node 1. Recurse to node 2, mark visited. Step 4: Process neighbor 0 of node 2, already visited, backtrack. Step 5: Backtrack through nodes 2,1,0. Note: This simple DFS doesn't detect cycles.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs & Dependencies
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of directed graphs, dependencies, and topological ordering.
ladders.explanation: A directed graph models prerequisites where an edge u→v means course u must be taken before course v. Topological ordering arranges courses so all prerequisites come before dependent courses. If a cycle exists, no valid ordering is possible.

examples.input: Courses: Math 101, Calculus, Physics. Prerequisites: Calculus depends on Math 101, Physics depends on Calculus.
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: Math101, Calculus, Physics. Step 2: Edge from Math101 to Calculus (Calculus requires Math101). Step 3: Edge from Calculus to Physics (Physics requires Calculus). Step 4: This forms a chain with no cycles. Valid order: Math101, Calculus, Physics.

examples.input: Courses: CS101, CS102. Prerequisites: CS101 depends on CS102, CS102 depends on CS101.
examples.trace: Step 1: Nodes: CS101, CS102. Step 2: Edge from CS102 to CS101 (CS101 requires CS102). Step 3: Edge from CS101 to CS102 (CS102 requires CS101). Step 4: This creates a cycle: CS101 → CS102 → CS101. No valid order exists.

testCases.input: Courses: A, B. Prerequisite: B depends on A.
Courses: X, Y, Z. Prerequisites: Y depends on X, Z depends on Y.
Courses: P, Q, R. Prerequisites: Q depends on P, R depends on Q, P depends on R.
Courses: C1, C2, C3. Prerequisites: C3 depends on C1, C3 depends on C2.
Courses: D, E, F. Prerequisites: E depends on D, F depends on D.
Courses: G, H. Prerequisites: G depends on H, H depends on G.
Courses: I, J, K. Prerequisites: J depends on I, K depends on J, K depends on I.
testCases.expected: Graph: I → J, I → K, J → K

testCases.input: Courses: L, M, N, O. Prerequisites: M depends on L, N depends on M, O depends on N.
Courses: S, T. Prerequisites: T depends on S, S depends on T.
